📚 Left Behind: The Global Crisis of Youth Out-of-School Rates

A UNICEF Data Report on Upper Secondary Education Exclusion

Author

Priyajbhai Pathak

Published

April 14, 2026

🌍 Introduction

Education is the cornerstone of human development — yet for hundreds of millions of young people around the world, the classroom door remains firmly shut. This report examines the out-of-school rate for youth of upper secondary school age — a critical indicator tracked by UNICEF that reveals the share of adolescents and young adults who are completely excluded from formal secondary education.

Upper secondary education (roughly ages 15–18) is a pivotal stage. It is the bridge between childhood learning and participation in the modern economy. Those who miss it face lifelong penalties: reduced earnings, poorer health outcomes, and diminished civic participation. When entire generations of young people in a country are excluded from secondary education, the consequences ripple through economic development, gender equality, and political stability for decades.

Note

What this indicator measures: The out-of-school rate is the percentage of youth who are of the official age to attend upper secondary school but are not enrolled in any level of formal education. A rate of 50% means half of an age cohort has dropped out of — or never entered — the school system.

This report draws on UNICEF microdata covering 113 countries across the period 2010–2020, merged with World Bank development indicators to explore the structural drivers of educational exclusion.

⚠️ Statement of Challenge

The data paints a stark picture. Across the 113 countries tracked by UNICEF, the average out-of-school rate for upper secondary youth stands at approximately 32% — meaning roughly one in three young people is excluded from this critical stage of education.

The challenge is profoundly unequal in its distribution:

  • Sub-Saharan Africa dominates the list of countries with the highest exclusion rates, with Niger, Mali, Burkina Faso, and Chad all exceeding 60%.
  • Gender gaps persist everywhere: in the majority of high-exclusion countries, girls are systematically less likely to be in school than boys.
  • Economic development is a strong — but not sufficient — predictor of inclusion: low GDP per capita is associated with high exclusion, but the relationship is not linear, and some low-income countries perform significantly better than peers.
  • The problem is not shrinking fast enough: time-series data from 2010–2020 shows only modest improvements in many of the worst-affected nations.

📊 The Data Story: Four Lenses on Educational Exclusion

🗺️ Chart 1 — World Map: Geographic Distribution of Out-of-School Rates

📌 Chart Insight: The world map reveals a stark geographic concentration of educational exclusion. Sub-Saharan Africa — particularly the Sahel belt — shows the darkest shading, with out-of-school rates frequently exceeding 60–80%. Parts of South Asia and South-East Asia also register moderate exclusion. Europe, much of Latin America, and East Asia present lighter tones, reflecting stronger school systems. International aid and development finance must be geographically targeted. Blanket global programmes will be dwarfed in impact by focused, country-level investments in the worst-affected regions, particularly across the Sahel.

📊 Chart 2 — Bar Chart: The 15 Countries with Highest Out-of-School Rates

📌 Chart Insight: Niger tops the list with an out-of-school rate exceeding 86% — meaning fewer than 1 in 7 young people of upper secondary age are in school. The top 15 is dominated by Sub-Saharan African nations, with Cambodia as the sole non-African entrant. All 15 countries record rates above 55%. These are not marginal cases requiring small adjustments — they represent systemic failure of educational infrastructure. Interventions must address the full supply chain of education: physical school infrastructure, teacher recruitment and pay, transport subsidies, and abolition of indirect costs such as uniforms and examination fees that price families out.

📈 Chart 3 — Scatterplot: GDP per Capita vs. Out-of-School Rate

📌 Chart Insight: The regression line confirms a strong negative relationship between national wealth and youth exclusion from secondary education — as GDP per capita rises, out-of-school rates fall sharply. However, the scatter is significant: some low-income countries dramatically outperform their wealth peers, demonstrating that policy choices matter independently of economic resources. Red dots (Sub-Saharan Africa) cluster in the high-exclusion, low-GDP quadrant, while blue and teal dots (Europe, East Asia) concentrate in the low-exclusion, high-GDP zone. While economic growth is a long-term enabler of educational access, country-level governance, investment prioritisation, and social policies explain the significant variance around the trend line. Donors should identify and study positive outliers — low-income countries with relatively low exclusion — and scale their approaches.

📉 Chart 4 — Time Series: Gender Gap Trends Over Time

📌 Chart Insight: In the hardest-hit countries, the gender gap in secondary education exclusion is persistent and substantial. Girls consistently record out-of-school rates 10–15 percentage points higher than boys throughout the 2010–2020 period. While both rates show a modest downward trend, the gap itself has not meaningfully closed — suggesting that generic education spending alone is insufficient to address the gendered barriers to schooling. Targeted programmes addressing the specific reasons girls leave school — early marriage, household labour demands, lack of female teachers, menstrual health infrastructure — are essential alongside general school expansion. Countries that have closed gender gaps in education (e.g., Rwanda, parts of Latin America) offer proven policy models.

🚨 Call to Action

The data is unambiguous. Millions of young people — disproportionately girls, disproportionately in Sub-Saharan Africa — are being permanently excluded from the educational foundation that determines life outcomes. This is not a natural state of affairs. It is the predictable consequence of chronic underfunding, weak governance, structural gender inequality, and policy neglect.

Every year a young person spends out of school is a year that cannot be recovered. The window for upper secondary education is narrow. By the time interventions are designed, funded, and implemented, many of the current cohort will have aged beyond the system entirely.

The international community — governments, multilateral institutions, NGOs, and the private sector — must treat this as the emergency it is:

  • 🔴 Immediate: Emergency funding for school retention programmes in the top 20 highest-exclusion countries
  • 🟡 Short-term (1–3 years): National education plans that explicitly target gender parity, with measurable milestones
  • 🟢 Long-term (5–10 years): Integration of education investment into poverty reduction frameworks, with cross-ministerial accountability

💡 Recommendations

Based on the analysis of UNICEF data across 113 countries, the following policy recommendations are offered:

  1. Increase domestic education budgets — Countries with out-of-school rates above 40% should be supported to achieve the international benchmark of at least 20% of national budgets allocated to education.

  2. Eliminate indirect costs of schooling — Uniforms, examination fees, and transport costs are proven barriers. Social protection programmes that subsidise these costs have demonstrated strong enrolment impacts in Kenya, Ethiopia, and Ghana.

  3. Deploy gender-targeted retention schemes — Conditional cash transfers to families of girls who remain enrolled through secondary school have succeeded in Bangladesh and Brazil. These should be scaled in high-exclusion African nations.

  4. Invest in teacher recruitment and quality — Many high-exclusion countries face acute teacher shortages, particularly in rural areas. International support for teacher training colleges and competitive public-sector teaching salaries is essential.

  5. Strengthen data collection and monitoring — Only 113 countries have data in this dataset, and many have significant temporal gaps. Robust annual monitoring of out-of-school rates — disaggregated by sex, geography, and disability — is prerequisite to accountability.

  6. Learn from positive outliers — Countries such as Rwanda and Cuba that achieve high enrolment despite limited GDP per capita represent laboratories of best practice. Their approaches — emphasising community schools, female teacher recruitment, and abolition of school fees — deserve systematic study and adaptation.

✅ Conclusion

The out-of-school crisis for upper secondary youth is one of the defining development challenges of our era. The data analysed in this report reveals both the scale of the problem and its structural drivers: poverty, geography, and gender inequality interact to exclude millions of young people from the education that would transform their life trajectories.

The encouraging signal in the data is that policy works. Wealthier countries keep more of their youth in school — but so do some lower-income countries that have made political choices to prioritise education. The gender gap, while persistent, has begun to narrow in some contexts. Progress is possible.

What is required is not technical innovation but political will: sustained investment, targeted programming, gender-sensitive design, and the accountability that comes from rigorous, disaggregated data. UNICEF, working with national governments and international partners, has a central role to play in driving this agenda.

“Every child has the right to an education. This is not a luxury — it is a necessity and a precondition of human dignity.” — UNICEF Education Programme

📚 Data Sources

The analysis in this report draws on the following datasets:

Dataset Description Link
UNICEF Education Indicator Out-of-school rate for youth of upper secondary school age, disaggregated by sex (2010–2020) UNICEF Data
World Bank Development Indicators GDP per capita, population, life expectancy, birth rates (1960–2023) World Bank Open Data
UNICEF Data Portal Global child and youth development statistics UNICEF Data Portal
UNESCO Institute for Statistics Global education monitoring methodology and definitions UIS Data
Tip

Reproducibility: All analysis code is embedded in this document and can be re-run using the Show Code toggles above each chart. The full dataset files (unicef_indicator_2.csv and unicef_metadata.csv) must be in the same directory as this .qmd file.

Data sourced from UNICEF Education Statistics and the World Bank Open Data platform.
Report produced for academic analysis. All rights to underlying data remain with the originating organisations.

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| echo: false

import polars as pl
import plotnine as p9
from plotnine import (
    ggplot, aes, geom_col, geom_point, geom_line, geom_smooth,
    geom_map, facet_wrap, labs, theme, theme_minimal, theme_void,
    element_text, element_rect, element_line, element_blank,
    scale_fill_gradient, scale_fill_gradientn, scale_color_manual,
    scale_x_continuous, scale_y_continuous, coord_flip,
    position_dodge, guides, guide_legend, annotate
)
import pandas as pd
import geopandas as gpd
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")
```

---

## 🌍 Introduction {#introduction}

Education is the cornerstone of human development — yet for hundreds of millions of young people around the world, the classroom door remains firmly shut. This report examines the **out-of-school rate for youth of upper secondary school age** — a critical indicator tracked by UNICEF that reveals the share of adolescents and young adults who are completely excluded from formal secondary education.

Upper secondary education (roughly ages 15–18) is a pivotal stage. It is the bridge between childhood learning and participation in the modern economy. Those who miss it face **lifelong penalties**: reduced earnings, poorer health outcomes, and diminished civic participation. When entire generations of young people in a country are excluded from secondary education, the consequences ripple through economic development, gender equality, and political stability for decades.

::: {.callout-note}
**What this indicator measures:** The out-of-school rate is the percentage of youth who are of the official age to attend upper secondary school but are **not enrolled** in any level of formal education. A rate of 50% means half of an age cohort has dropped out of — or never entered — the school system.
:::

This report draws on UNICEF microdata covering **113 countries** across the period **2010–2020**, merged with World Bank development indicators to explore the structural drivers of educational exclusion.

---

## ⚠️ Statement of Challenge {#challenge}

```{python}
#| label: data-loading
#| code-summary: "Load and clean data"

# ── Load raw files ────────────────────────────────────────────
indicator = pl.read_csv("unicef_indicator_2.csv")
metadata  = pl.read_csv("unicef_metadata.csv")

# ── Rename metadata columns for easier use ───────────────────
metadata = metadata.rename({
    "Population, total":                        "population",
    "GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)":       "gdp_per_capita",
    "Life expectancy at birth, total (years)":  "life_expectancy",
    "Birth rate, crude (per 1,000 people)":     "birth_rate",
    "Military expenditure (% of GDP)":          "military_pct_gdp",
    "GNI (current US$)":                        "gni",
    "GDP growth (annual %)":                    "gdp_growth",
    "Inflation, consumer prices (annual %)":    "inflation",
    "Fossil fuel energy consumption (% of total)": "fossil_fuel_pct",
    "Hospital beds (per 1,000 people)":         "hospital_beds",
})

# ── Filter to Total sex for headline analyses ─────────────────
ind_total = (
    indicator
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .rename({"time_period": "year", "obs_value": "oos_rate"})
    .with_columns(pl.col("year").cast(pl.Int64))
)

# ── Merge with metadata ───────────────────────────────────────
merged = ind_total.join(
    metadata,
    on=["country", "alpha_3_code", "year"],
    how="left"
)

# ── Merge for gendered analyses ───────────────────────────────
ind_gender = (
    indicator
    .filter(pl.col("sex").is_in(["Female", "Male"]))
    .rename({"time_period": "year", "obs_value": "oos_rate"})
    .with_columns(pl.col("year").cast(pl.Int64))
)
```

The data paints a stark picture. Across the 113 countries tracked by UNICEF, the **average out-of-school rate for upper secondary youth stands at approximately 32%** — meaning roughly one in three young people is excluded from this critical stage of education.

The challenge is profoundly unequal in its distribution:

- **Sub-Saharan Africa** dominates the list of countries with the highest exclusion rates, with Niger, Mali, Burkina Faso, and Chad all exceeding 60%.
- **Gender gaps persist everywhere**: in the majority of high-exclusion countries, girls are systematically less likely to be in school than boys.
- **Economic development is a strong — but not sufficient — predictor** of inclusion: low GDP per capita is associated with high exclusion, but the relationship is not linear, and some low-income countries perform significantly better than peers.
- **The problem is not shrinking fast enough**: time-series data from 2010–2020 shows only modest improvements in many of the worst-affected nations.

```{python}
#| label: summary-stats
#| code-summary: "Compute descriptive statistics"

# ── Regional mapping ─────────────────────────────────────────
region_map = {
    "Niger": "Sub-Saharan Africa", "Mali": "Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "Burkina Faso": "Sub-Saharan Africa", "Chad": "Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "Madagascar": "Sub-Saharan Africa", "Guinea": "Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "Uganda": "Sub-Saharan Africa", "South Sudan": "Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "Tanzania, United Republic of": "Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "Mozambique": "Sub-Saharan Africa", "Ethiopia": "Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "Nigeria": "Sub-Saharan Africa", "Ghana": "Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "Kenya": "Sub-Saharan Africa", "Rwanda": "Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "Cameroon": "Sub-Saharan Africa", "Senegal": "Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "Malawi": "Sub-Saharan Africa", "Zambia": "Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "Zimbabwe": "Sub-Saharan Africa", "Togo": "Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "Benin": "Sub-Saharan Africa", "Guinea-Bissau": "Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "Cambodia": "East Asia & Pacific", "Myanmar": "East Asia & Pacific",
    "Lao People's Democratic Republic": "East Asia & Pacific",
    "Philippines": "East Asia & Pacific", "Indonesia": "East Asia & Pacific",
    "Papua New Guinea": "East Asia & Pacific",
    "Afghanistan": "South Asia", "Pakistan": "South Asia",
    "Bangladesh": "South Asia", "Nepal": "South Asia",
    "India": "South Asia", "Bhutan": "South Asia",
    "Iraq": "Middle East & North Africa", "Morocco": "Middle East & North Africa",
    "Algeria": "Middle East & North Africa", "Egypt": "Middle East & North Africa",
    "Tunisia": "Middle East & North Africa", "Libya": "Middle East & North Africa",
    "Jordan": "Middle East & North Africa", "Yemen": "Middle East & North Africa",
    "Guatemala": "Latin America & Caribbean", "Honduras": "Latin America & Caribbean",
    "Nicaragua": "Latin America & Caribbean", "Haiti": "Latin America & Caribbean",
    "El Salvador": "Latin America & Caribbean", "Paraguay": "Latin America & Caribbean",
    "Bolivia (Plurinational State of)": "Latin America & Caribbean",
    "Colombia": "Latin America & Caribbean", "Ecuador": "Latin America & Caribbean",
    "Peru": "Latin America & Caribbean", "Brazil": "Latin America & Caribbean",
    "Mexico": "Latin America & Caribbean",
    "Albania": "Europe & Central Asia", "Moldova": "Europe & Central Asia",
    "Georgia": "Europe & Central Asia", "Ukraine": "Europe & Central Asia",
    "Armenia": "Europe & Central Asia", "Kyrgyzstan": "Europe & Central Asia",
    "Tajikistan": "Europe & Central Asia", "Kosovo": "Europe & Central Asia",
}

# Assign regions, default to "Other" for unmapped
ind_total_pd = ind_total.to_pandas()
ind_total_pd["region"] = ind_total_pd["country"].map(region_map).fillna("Other")

# ── Top 15 countries (most recent year available) ─────────────
latest = (
    ind_total
    .sort("year", descending=True)
    .group_by("country")
    .first()
    .sort("oos_rate", descending=True)
)

top15 = latest.head(15).to_pandas()
```

---

## 📊 The Data Story: Four Lenses on Educational Exclusion {#data-story}

### 🗺️ Chart 1 — World Map: Geographic Distribution of Out-of-School Rates

```{python}
#| label: fig-worldmap
#| fig-cap: "Out-of-school rate for upper secondary youth by country (most recent available year, 2010–2020)"
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 6
#| code-summary: "World map visualisation"

# ── Prepare country-level data (most recent year per country) ─
world_data = latest.to_pandas()[["alpha_3_code", "oos_rate", "year"]].copy()

# ── Load world boundaries with GeoPandas (Natural Earth) ──────
world = gpd.read_file(
    "https://naturalearth.s3.amazonaws.com/110m_cultural/ne_110m_admin_0_countries.zip"
)

# ── Merge by ISO3 code (robust, avoids name reconciliation) ───
world_merged = world.merge(
    world_data,
    left_on="ADM0_A3",
    right_on="alpha_3_code",
    how="left"
)

# ── Plot with plotnine geom_map (works with GeoDataFrame) ─────
map_plot = (
    ggplot(world_merged, aes(fill="oos_rate"))
    + geom_map(color="white", size=0.15)
    + scale_fill_gradientn(
        colors=["#cce5ff", "#66b3ff", "#1a5fa8", "#0d2a5e", "#05102a"],
        na_value="#e8e8e8",
        name="OOS Rate (%)",
        limits=[0, 90],
        breaks=[0, 20, 40, 60, 80],
    )
    + labs(
        title="Youth Out-of-School Rate: Upper Secondary Education",
        subtitle="Darker shading = higher proportion of youth excluded from school | Grey = no data",
        caption="Source: UNICEF, most recent year 2010–2020",
    )
    + theme_void()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=14, weight="bold", color="#1a3a8c"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="#445577"),
        plot_caption=element_text(size=8, color="#778899"),
        legend_title=element_text(size=9, weight="bold"),
        legend_background=element_rect(fill="#f5faff", color="#c5ddf5"),
        figure_size=(12, 6),
    )
)

# IMPORTANT: return the plot object (do NOT print)
map_plot
```

::: {.chart-insight}
**📌 Chart Insight:** The world map reveals a stark geographic concentration of educational exclusion. Sub-Saharan Africa — particularly the Sahel belt — shows the darkest shading, with out-of-school rates frequently exceeding 60–80%. Parts of South Asia and South-East Asia also register moderate exclusion. Europe, much of Latin America, and East Asia present lighter tones, reflecting stronger school systems. International aid and development finance must be geographically targeted. Blanket global programmes will be dwarfed in impact by focused, country-level investments in the worst-affected regions, particularly across the Sahel.
:::

---

### 📊 Chart 2 — Bar Chart: The 15 Countries with Highest Out-of-School Rates

```{python}
#| label: fig-barchart
#| fig-cap: "Top 15 countries by youth out-of-school rate at upper secondary level (most recent available year)"
#| fig-width: 11
#| fig-height: 7
#| code-summary: "Bar chart of top 15 countries"

# ── Prepare data ──────────────────────────────────────────────
bar_df = top15.copy()
bar_df["label"] = bar_df.apply(lambda r: f"{r['oos_rate']:.1f}%  ({int(r['year'])})", axis=1)

# Colour scale: red-to-dark-blue by rank
bar_df = bar_df.sort_values("oos_rate")
bar_df["country_ordered"] = pd.Categorical(bar_df["country"], categories=bar_df["country"].tolist(), ordered=True)

bar_plot = (
    ggplot(bar_df, aes(x="country_ordered", y="oos_rate", fill="oos_rate"))
    + geom_col(width=0.72, show_legend=False)
    + geom_point(aes(y="oos_rate"), color="#ffffff", size=0, show_legend=False)  # spacer
    + coord_flip()
    + scale_fill_gradientn(
        colors=["#4a90d9", "#1a5fa8", "#0d2a5e"],
        limits=[0, 95],
    )
    + scale_y_continuous(limits=[0, 100], breaks=list(range(0, 101, 20)),
                         labels=lambda l: [f"{int(x)}%" for x in l])
    + annotate("text", x=bar_df["country_ordered"].tolist(),
               y=bar_df["oos_rate"] + 1.5,
               label=bar_df["oos_rate"].apply(lambda v: f"{v:.1f}%").tolist(),
               ha="left", size=8, color="#1a3a8c")
    + labs(
        title="Countries with the Highest Youth Out-of-School Rates",
        subtitle="Upper secondary education | Most recent year available (2010–2020)",
        x="",
        y="Out-of-School Rate (%)",
        caption="Source: UNICEF Education Statistics",
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, weight="bold", color="#1a3a8c"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="#445577"),
        plot_caption=element_text(size=8, color="#778899"),
        axis_text_y=element_text(size=9, color="#1a2a4a"),
        axis_text_x=element_text(size=9),
        panel_grid_major_y=element_blank(),
        panel_grid_minor_x=element_blank(),
        figure_size=(11, 7),
    )
)
bar_plot
```

::: {.chart-insight}
**📌 Chart Insight:** Niger tops the list with an out-of-school rate exceeding 86% — meaning fewer than 1 in 7 young people of upper secondary age are in school. The top 15 is dominated by Sub-Saharan African nations, with Cambodia as the sole non-African entrant. All 15 countries record rates above 55%. These are not marginal cases requiring small adjustments — they represent systemic failure of educational infrastructure. Interventions must address the full supply chain of education: physical school infrastructure, teacher recruitment and pay, transport subsidies, and abolition of indirect costs such as uniforms and examination fees that price families out.
:::

---

### 📈 Chart 3 — Scatterplot: GDP per Capita vs. Out-of-School Rate

```{python}
#| label: fig-scatter
#| fig-cap: "Relationship between GDP per capita and youth out-of-school rate, with OLS regression line (countries with data on both variables)"
#| fig-width: 11
#| fig-height: 7
#| code-summary: "Scatterplot with linear regression"

# ── Merge indicator with metadata for GDP data ────────────────
scatter_df = merged.to_pandas().dropna(subset=["gdp_per_capita", "oos_rate"])
scatter_df = scatter_df[scatter_df["gdp_per_capita"] > 0]

# Log-transform GDP per capita for readability
import numpy as np
scatter_df["log_gdp"] = np.log10(scatter_df["gdp_per_capita"])

# Assign regions for colour
scatter_df["plot_region"] = scatter_df["country"].map(region_map).fillna("Other")

region_colours = {
    "Sub-Saharan Africa":        "#c0392b",
    "South Asia":                "#e67e22",
    "East Asia & Pacific":       "#27ae60",
    "Latin America & Caribbean": "#8e44ad",
    "Middle East & North Africa":"#2980b9",
    "Europe & Central Asia":     "#1a3a8c",
    "Other":                     "#95a5a6",
}

scatter_plot = (
    ggplot(scatter_df, aes(x="log_gdp", y="oos_rate", color="plot_region"))
    + geom_point(alpha=0.65, size=2.8)
    + geom_smooth(aes(group=1), method="lm", color="#1a3a8c", fill="#b0d4f0", size=1.2)
    + scale_color_manual(values=region_colours, name="Region")
    + scale_x_continuous(
        breaks=[2, 2.5, 3, 3.5, 4, 4.5],
        labels=["$100", "$316", "$1k", "$3.2k", "$10k", "$32k"],
    )
    + scale_y_continuous(breaks=list(range(0, 101, 20)),
                         labels=lambda l: [f"{int(x)}%" for x in l])
    + labs(
        title="Wealthier Countries Keep More Youth in School",
        subtitle="GDP per capita (log scale) vs. out-of-school rate | Each point = one country-year",
        x="GDP per Capita (log scale, constant 2015 USD)",
        y="Out-of-School Rate (%)",
        caption="Source: UNICEF Education Statistics & World Bank Development Indicators",
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, weight="bold", color="#1a3a8c"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="#445577"),
        plot_caption=element_text(size=8, color="#778899"),
        legend_title=element_text(size=9, weight="bold"),
        legend_background=element_rect(fill="#f5faff", color="#c5ddf5"),
        figure_size=(11, 7),
    )
)
scatter_plot
```

::: {.chart-insight}
**📌 Chart Insight:** The regression line confirms a strong negative relationship between national wealth and youth exclusion from secondary education — as GDP per capita rises, out-of-school rates fall sharply. However, the scatter is significant: **some low-income countries dramatically outperform their wealth peers**, demonstrating that policy choices matter independently of economic resources. Red dots (Sub-Saharan Africa) cluster in the high-exclusion, low-GDP quadrant, while blue and teal dots (Europe, East Asia) concentrate in the low-exclusion, high-GDP zone. While economic growth is a long-term enabler of educational access, country-level governance, investment prioritisation, and social policies explain the significant variance around the trend line. Donors should identify and study positive outliers — low-income countries with relatively low exclusion — and scale their approaches.
:::

---

### 📉 Chart 4 — Time Series: Gender Gap Trends Over Time

```{python}
#| label: fig-timeseries
#| fig-cap: "Average out-of-school rate by sex over time (2010–2020) for countries in the highest-exclusion quartile"
#| fig-width: 11
#| fig-height: 6
#| code-summary: "Time-series chart by gender"

# ── Identify highest-exclusion countries (top quartile) ──────
high_excl_countries = (
    ind_total
    .group_by("country")
    .agg(pl.col("oos_rate").mean().alias("mean_rate"))
    .filter(pl.col("mean_rate") >= pl.col("mean_rate").quantile(0.75))
    .select("country")
    .to_series()
    .to_list()
)

# ── Filter gendered data to those countries ───────────────────
gender_ts = (
    ind_gender
    .filter(pl.col("country").is_in(high_excl_countries))
    .group_by(["year", "sex"])
    .agg(pl.col("oos_rate").mean().alias("mean_oos"))
    .sort("year")
    .to_pandas()
)

sex_colours = {"Female": "#c0392b", "Male": "#2980b9"}

ts_plot = (
    ggplot(gender_ts, aes(x="year", y="mean_oos", color="sex", group="sex"))
    + geom_line(size=1.6)
    + geom_point(size=3.5, fill="white", stroke=1.5)
    + scale_color_manual(values=sex_colours, name="Sex")
    + scale_x_continuous(
        breaks=list(range(2010, 2021)),
        labels=[str(y) for y in range(2010, 2021)],
    )
    + scale_y_continuous(
        limits=[30, 80],
        breaks=list(range(30, 81, 10)),
        labels=lambda l: [f"{int(x)}%" for x in l],
    )
    + annotate("text", x=2019.3, y=65.5, label="Female", color="#c0392b",
           size=10, fontweight="bold")
+ annotate("text", x=2019.3, y=54.0, label="Male",   color="#2980b9",
           size=10, fontweight="bold")
    + labs(
        title="The Gender Gap: Girls Are More Likely to Be Left Behind",
        subtitle="Average out-of-school rate in highest-exclusion countries (2010–2020) | Female vs. Male",
        x="Year",
        y="Average Out-of-School Rate (%)",
        caption="Source: UNICEF Education Statistics | Highest-exclusion quartile of countries",
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, weight="bold", color="#1a3a8c"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="#445577"),
        plot_caption=element_text(size=8, color="#778899"),
        panel_grid_minor=element_blank(),
        legend_position="none",
        figure_size=(11, 6),
    )
)
ts_plot
```

::: {.chart-insight}
**📌 Chart Insight:** In the hardest-hit countries, the gender gap in secondary education exclusion is persistent and substantial. Girls consistently record out-of-school rates 10–15 percentage points higher than boys throughout the 2010–2020 period. While both rates show a modest downward trend, the gap itself has **not meaningfully closed** — suggesting that generic education spending alone is insufficient to address the gendered barriers to schooling. Targeted programmes addressing the specific reasons girls leave school — early marriage, household labour demands, lack of female teachers, menstrual health infrastructure — are essential alongside general school expansion. Countries that have closed gender gaps in education (e.g., Rwanda, parts of Latin America) offer proven policy models.
:::

---

## 🚨 Call to Action {#call-to-action}

The data is unambiguous. Millions of young people — disproportionately girls, disproportionately in Sub-Saharan Africa — are being permanently excluded from the educational foundation that determines life outcomes. This is not a natural state of affairs. It is the predictable consequence of chronic underfunding, weak governance, structural gender inequality, and policy neglect.

**Every year a young person spends out of school is a year that cannot be recovered.** The window for upper secondary education is narrow. By the time interventions are designed, funded, and implemented, many of the current cohort will have aged beyond the system entirely.

The international community — governments, multilateral institutions, NGOs, and the private sector — must treat this as the emergency it is:

- 🔴 **Immediate:** Emergency funding for school retention programmes in the top 20 highest-exclusion countries
- 🟡 **Short-term (1–3 years):** National education plans that explicitly target gender parity, with measurable milestones
- 🟢 **Long-term (5–10 years):** Integration of education investment into poverty reduction frameworks, with cross-ministerial accountability

---

## 💡 Recommendations {#recommendations}

Based on the analysis of UNICEF data across 113 countries, the following policy recommendations are offered:

1. **Increase domestic education budgets** — Countries with out-of-school rates above 40% should be supported to achieve the international benchmark of **at least 20% of national budgets** allocated to education.

2. **Eliminate indirect costs of schooling** — Uniforms, examination fees, and transport costs are proven barriers. Social protection programmes that subsidise these costs have demonstrated strong enrolment impacts in Kenya, Ethiopia, and Ghana.

3. **Deploy gender-targeted retention schemes** — Conditional cash transfers to families of girls who remain enrolled through secondary school have succeeded in Bangladesh and Brazil. These should be scaled in high-exclusion African nations.

4. **Invest in teacher recruitment and quality** — Many high-exclusion countries face acute teacher shortages, particularly in rural areas. International support for teacher training colleges and competitive public-sector teaching salaries is essential.

5. **Strengthen data collection and monitoring** — Only 113 countries have data in this dataset, and many have significant temporal gaps. Robust annual monitoring of out-of-school rates — disaggregated by sex, geography, and disability — is prerequisite to accountability.

6. **Learn from positive outliers** — Countries such as Rwanda and Cuba that achieve high enrolment despite limited GDP per capita represent laboratories of best practice. Their approaches — emphasising community schools, female teacher recruitment, and abolition of school fees — deserve systematic study and adaptation.

---

## ✅ Conclusion {#conclusion}

The out-of-school crisis for upper secondary youth is one of the defining development challenges of our era. The data analysed in this report reveals both the scale of the problem and its structural drivers: poverty, geography, and gender inequality interact to exclude millions of young people from the education that would transform their life trajectories.

The encouraging signal in the data is that **policy works**. Wealthier countries keep more of their youth in school — but so do some lower-income countries that have made political choices to prioritise education. The gender gap, while persistent, has begun to narrow in some contexts. Progress is possible.

What is required is not technical innovation but political will: sustained investment, targeted programming, gender-sensitive design, and the accountability that comes from rigorous, disaggregated data. UNICEF, working with national governments and international partners, has a central role to play in driving this agenda.

> *"Every child has the right to an education. This is not a luxury — it is a necessity and a precondition of human dignity."*
> — UNICEF Education Programme

---

## 📚 Data Sources {#data-sources}

The analysis in this report draws on the following datasets:

| Dataset | Description | Link |
|---------|-------------|------|
| UNICEF Education Indicator | Out-of-school rate for youth of upper secondary school age, disaggregated by sex (2010–2020) | [UNICEF Data](https://data.unicef.org/topic/education/overview/) |
| World Bank Development Indicators | GDP per capita, population, life expectancy, birth rates (1960–2023) | [World Bank Open Data](https://data.worldbank.org/indicator) |
| UNICEF Data Portal | Global child and youth development statistics | [UNICEF Data Portal](https://data.unicef.org) |
| UNESCO Institute for Statistics | Global education monitoring methodology and definitions | [UIS Data](http://data.uis.unesco.org) |

::: {.callout-tip}
**Reproducibility:** All analysis code is embedded in this document and can be re-run using the **Show Code** toggles above each chart. The full dataset files (`unicef_indicator_2.csv` and `unicef_metadata.csv`) must be in the same directory as this `.qmd` file.
:::
